entry.title: Boise-based Erstad bought by Salt Lake Firm
entry.summary: FFKR Architects has acquired the Boise-based architecture and interior design firm, Erstad, which has served the Treasure Valley for 28 years. A spokesperson for Erstad told BoiseDev that their Boise office will continue to operate as usual and that all employees have been retained. FFKR has offices in Boise, Salt Lake City, St. George, and Tempe, Arizona. “This merger brings together the strengths of both teams, expanding their ability to serve clients and communities,” Erstad said in a news release. “By working as one, Erstad and FFKR are positioned to take on more complex projects, offer broader services, and make a greater impact across the region.” A spokesperson for Erstad told BoiseDev that over time, the two brands are eventually expected to merge into one identity under FFKR. Michael Leishman will continue to serve as President of FFKR, while Andy Erstad will serve as Senior Principal and Vice President, and Katrina Kulm as a Principal and member of FFKR’s Executive Committee. Erstad and Kulm have also assumed ownership roles within FFKR. “The merger reflects a step forward for both firms — one that enhances what already exists while opening the door to new opportunities. Together, Erstad and FFKR look forward to continuing our legacy of bringing design excellence and innovation to our clients and communities.” Erstad has dozens of projects under their belt, including 8th Street Marketplace in downtown Boise, and the Boardwalk Apartment and Residences in Garden City, while FFKR has worked on projects in Boise, including the Stueckle Sky Center and Stadium Addition at Boise State University. Advertise

entry.title: You Asked: What happened to ‘Boise icon’ MK steam shovel?
entry.summary: A vintage steam shovel that has been on prominent display in Boise for decades and was once named one of the 150 defining icons of the city, recently disappeared from prominent view. The Bucyrus-Erie B-3 steam shovel sat on a perch in front of the headquarters of Morrison-Knudsen Co. at Broadway Ave. and Park Blvd. for decades. Morrison-Knudsen, or M-K, started in 1912, when Harry Morrison and Morris Knudsen worked on a project together in Grand View, Idaho. It grew into one of the world’s foremost construction firms, building the Hoover Dam, Kennedy Space Center, and many more. In 1982, eM-Kayan, the MK employee magazine, noted in 1980 that a crew had refurbished the shovel for display. “A vintage 1931 coal-fired steam shovel recently was restored to like-new condition at M-K’s Boise Industrial Shops for permanent display at the company’s headquarters complex along with a number of other items of equipment from the company’s early days,” the magazine wrote. Idaho Statesman reporter Anna Webb included the shovel on a list of 150 icons to celebrate the city’s sesquicentennial, alongside Betty the Washer Woman, hot air balloons, and the Boise River. The steam shovel’s fate was indirectly altered when Bill Agee was hired as MK CEO in 1988. He made a series of moves that ultimately drove the company into bankruptcy and triggered his firing in early 1995. In 1996, Washington Construction purchased MK out of bankruptcy for $380 million. In July 2000, MK’s corporate name was changed to Washington Group International after acquiring portions of Westinghouse Electric Company and Raytheon. The Raytheon deal led the company to bankruptcy again. In 2007, URS purchased WGI, and finally, Los Angeles-based AECOM bought URS. By 2017, the remnants of MK in Idaho were gone. In 2012, with the former MK headquarters at Broadway Ave. and Park Blvd. no longer a hub of activity for the company, the steam shovel was donated to the Idaho Transportation Department. Crews from Inland Crane hoisted the massive shovel from its perch on Broadway, trucked it through downtown, and placed it on a custom-built pad in front of the ITD headquarters on State St. A decade later, in the early days of 2022, a burst pipe flooded the building, and employees relocated to the state’s campus on Chinden Blvd. The state started work on a new building for ITD on Chinden and sold the State St. property to Boise development firm Hawkins. But Hawkins had a long due diligence period in its deal to buy the State St. property, and the legislature intervened and killed the deal. Now, crews are in the process of attempting to renovate the State St. building for ITD to move back. Earlier this spring, the steam shovel disappeared. We need your support. BoiseDev provides fact-based reporting to our community free. It''s only possible with support from readers. Make a donation of any size here Or become a member for great benefits. John Tomlinson, Communication Manager for ITD, said the shovel was moved to aid in the work on the building. The elevator in the building is not operating, so crews set up a series of trash chutes that linked the front of the building to trucks to haul away debris. The steam shovel was in the path needed for the chutes, he said. So a crane was brought in, and for $15,385, it was moved from its perch in front of the building to an area behind a fence off to the side of the building. As for the steam shovel’s fate? “We have talked to the original owners of it, and are trying to figure out what we are going to do with it. We do not have a plan for it currently,” he said, noting it could be put back to its location in front of the building in the future. Advertise

entry.title: Boise company known for Ashton Jeanty trading cards lands sale to Reddit co-founder’s company
entry.summary: ONIT, a Boise-based company that specializes in officially licensed college sports trading cards, has been acquired by Reddit co-founder Alexis Ohanian’s venture capital firm Seven Seven Six. “ONIT is a natural fit with Seven Seven Six,” Ohanian said. “It’s an opportunity to create a vehicle for underserved sports to reach their fans through sports cards, a product category I’ve been passionate about since I was a kid. ONIT understands something fundamental about fandom and collecting that others have missed: it’s about community. The trading card hobby is growing fast, and ONIT’s opportunity is to bring entirely new collectors into the space – not just serve the ones who already exist – by making collecting more accessible.” In the 2024-2025 school year, ONIT worked with more than 8,000 athletes on 212 teams across 67 Division I schools, including Boise State. The opportunity allows student-athletes to earn royalties through Name, Image, and Likeness (NIL). BoiseDev previously reported ONIT started as a real estate media and marketing company in 2021. Mike Walsh, ONIT’s chief operating officer, told BoiseDev the company’s trading card division started in 2022. Walsh said in 2024 that ONIT’s parent company, Skyview Media, divested the real estate marketing segment and began focusing on college sports collectibles. Boise State’s partnership with ONIT began in 2022, when the Bronco football program became the first in the country to have its entire team represented in a trading card set. The university also partnered with Jacksons Food Stores as part of the project. In 2023, the program expanded to include every team at Boise State, making it the first school in the country to do so since NIL’s inception. In November 2025, Boise State’s ESports program became the first collegiate ESports program in the country to have trading cards from ONIT. ONIT has previously worked with The College of Idaho and Northwest Nazarene University. Advertise

entry.title: I-84 upgrades: Meters, new lanes, aux lanes could be part of $300 million+ in construction
entry.summary: Auxiliary lanes, ramp metering, widening projects, and hundreds of millions of dollars in upgrades could eventually come to I-84 as the Idaho Transportation Department attempts to keep things moving. It’s no secret that getting on I-84 in the valley at 5 p.m. will usually find commuters sitting in traffic. With more people moving to the valley each year, traffic congestion is projected to get worse. According to District Engineer Jason Brinkman, by 2055, if nothing is done, travel times are expected to nearly double. And that’s not the only impact. “Not only do the travel times during the peak hour double, but the peak hour becomes the peak hours,” Brinkman said in his report to the ITD board on Wednesday. He said the congestion would continue to bleed further out onto local roadways. That’s why, Brinkman said, ITD decided to look at how it might make the interstate run smoother through the I-84 mobility study. The I-84 mobility study was done with the goal of finding ways to keep traffic moving at a consistent rate, rather than simply increasing the roadway’s capacity. Brinkman says ITD wants to ultimately avoid having certain areas where cars can drive at the speed limit and other areas where traffic flow quickly slows to stop-and-go at chokepoints. “We have to accept some congestion. 50 or 60 miles per hour during an hour or two in the morning or the evening is not a problem if it operates consistently at that,” Brinkman said. “The goal of this study is not to identify ways to build general capacity. There is a little bit of an axiom that you can never entirely build your way out of capacity in a situation like this.” The study looked at several scenarios and solutions to help improve traffic flow. A buildout of any of the interventions would have to go through the design process and secure funding before any construction could occur. One option was to keep the interstate to four lanes each way but add auxiliary lanes that would connect on-ramps to the next off-ramps, as BoiseDev reported it did between Eagle Rd. and Meridian Rd. These lanes would help by eliminating the need for cars going short distances to merge into other lanes. The auxiliary lanes would be converted from the existing shoulders. The study also looked at ramp metering, which is where traffic signals are placed on freeway on-ramps to control the frequency at which vehicles merge into highway traffic. Brinkman said ramp metering is fairly low-cost and easy to implement. He said, however, that he was unsure of public acceptance of the concept and that ITD staff would not feel comfortable implementing this approach without “at least approaching the legislature for enabling legislation that’s specifically named ‘ramp metering.’” We need your support. BoiseDev provides fact-based reporting to our community free. It''s only possible with support from readers. Make a donation of any size here Or become a member for great benefits. While the study found that ramp metering and four lanes plus auxiliary lanes were effective in easing congestion for the eastbound commute, Brinkman said for the westbound commute, the auxiliary lane impact was minimal and the ramp metering did “almost nothing.” The engineer told the board that increasing the interstate to five lanes each way with auxiliary lanes was found to solve the congestion problem almost entirely. But, Brinkman said, it comes at a cost ITD is not willing to pay. “It comes at a very high cost and very long implementation time,” Brinkman said. “Five lanes plus auxiliary lanes would require the replacement of almost every one of the brand new interchanges and structures in the corridor, which we believe is a step too far.” However, the study found that for some areas, the only option for relief is to build more lanes. Brinkman pointed to the I-184/I-84 merge at the Flying Wye as the best example of this. Given this data, ITD staff is recommending a hybrid plan. The department wants to increase I-84 to five lanes plus auxiliary lanes each way between the Flying Wye and Eagle Rd. Then, the interstate would move down to four lanes plus the auxiliaries everywhere else. Brinkman said the existing bridges and the design for the new bridge at Five Mile Rd. would fit this new configuration with “a minor exception” at Cloverdale Rd. that would allow them to “just squeeze through.” All told, Brinkman said the anticipated cost for the project would be $300-400 million and would take four to eight years to build out. The Flying Wye to Eagle Rd. segment would be the most expensive part of the project, as Brinkman said it would require right-of-way acquisition, sound walls, and drainage improvements. Right of way is land owned and maintained by a jurisdiction, often a state, city or highway district. The land is reserved for public use, like roadways. Often, when an area requires roadway improvements, such as widening, and the responsible jurisdiction needs additional space, they begin a right-of-way acquisition process. This includes working with property owners to purchase the needed property. ITD staff’s estimated time of build for these projects is the mid 2030s but is “very much dependent on funding.” The ITD Board approved $20 million later in the meeting to fund early design for “high priority” portions of the project. ITD’s Amy Schroeder said the $20 million was “part of a larger funding plan for the I-84 mobility improvements.” Mobility improvements between the Flying Wye and Garrity Blvd. interchanges are not the only I-84 upgrades ITD is considering for the future. Brinkman told the ITD board that the District 3 team is also moving forward with a widening study of I-84 between Boise and Mountain Home.

entry.title: You Asked: What’s happening at Capital High School?
entry.summary: Readers have reached out to BoiseDev to ask what’s going on at Capital High School. Driving by, you can spot a lot of construction happening in the parking lot in front of the school and behind the school in the softball fields. Dan Hollar, a spokesperson for the Boise School District, told BoiseDev they are building a new parking lot and redesigning the traffic flow system, which will include a separate bus loop and a designated parent drop-off spot. There will also be a new courtyard and improved entry and exit points. Other work includes new gas lines, water connections, and sewer tie-ins. The front of the building is closed, and the front parking lot is expected to be finished in time for the start of the school year on August 12. Hollar also said part of the project includes relocating the school’s softball fields. According to renderings filed with the City of Boise in May 2025, one field will be moved from the northeast to the northwest, butting up against the baseball field, while the other appears to be eliminated. BoiseDev previously reported the Tuttle-Spauding Pathway, linking Milwaukee Street to Cole Road, will be constructed south of the high school between the school district property and Milwaukee Park. Bonnie Shelton with Boise Parks and Recreation told BoiseDev that construction will likely start in mid-October and should be finished early next year. The Boise School District and the City of Boise negotiated how the pathway would impact the sports facilities at Capital. Then-Boise Parks and Recreation Director Doug Holloway told BoiseDev the pathway would clip “a small piece” of the school’s JV softball field. After additional planning, the district decided to use the existing field and take part in doubleheaders, instead of each team playing at the same time. Hollar told BoiseDev the field will also have lights. Several projects are taking place inside the school as well. The auxiliary gym will soon have new bleachers, and the school is replacing its boiler. We need your support. BoiseDev provides fact-based reporting to our community free. It''s only possible with support from readers. Make a donation of any size here Or become a member for great benefits. “Earlier this year, Boise School District launched a comprehensive Facilities Master Planning process designed to assess the condition, capacity, educational adequacy, and future needs of all district facilities,” Hollar said. “The effort includes a review of enrollment and demographic trends, facility conditions, educational programming needs, and funding strategies, and will ultimately guide facility investments and capital improvement priorities for the next 10 to 15 years. This public process is intended to create a comprehensive vision for our future facilities portfolio while helping identify and prioritize future maintenance, modernization, and construction needs.” Advertise

entry.title: You Asked: Why is an old Boise house once slated for demolition sitting on jacks?
entry.summary: If you’ve driven down Broadway Avenue in Boise, you may have noticed construction activity around one of Boise’s oldest homes. The Nathan Smith House, a 126-year-old home at 2315 S. Broadway Avenue, is in the process of being moved to a new location on its property as part of a major restoration effort. BoiseDev readers reached out after spotting work underway at the site. So what’s happening? Back in 2025, The Idaho Statesman reported that the Nathan Smith House was slated for demolition. But today, the historic home is still standing, and now it is literally on the move. The home, built in 1900, was designed by Boise architectural firm Tourtellotte & Hummel, the same firm responsible for the Idaho State Capitol building. It was added to the National Register of Historic Places in 1983. The Idaho State Historical Society notes that “Nathan Smith was a fruit grower living just south of what was then the village of South Boise. His house reportedly cost $3,000.” Originally constructed on what was then a prune orchard south of Boise, the home is known for its distinct architecture, including a second-story balcony above the front porch, decorative window details, and stonework sourced from local materials. According to Ben Semple, landscape architect with Rodney Evans + Partners, the home’s sandstone foundation was originally quarried from Table Rock, while the river rock veneer used on portions of the exterior came from the Boise River. “It’s one of the more flamboyant home designs that Tourtellotte & Hummel did early on,” Semple said. The home is currently surrounded by newly constructed townhomes that are expected to be completed later this summer. We need your support. BoiseDev provides fact-based reporting to our community free. It''s only possible with support from readers. Make a donation of any size here Or become a member for great benefits. The home isn’t leaving the property. Instead, owner Huron Properties LLC, is relocating it closer to Broadway Avenue. Aaron Ellsworth, manager of Huron Properties LLC, said the goal is to make the historic structure more visible while preserving it for future generations. “We all want to see the history preserved,” Ellsworth said. “We decided that the best way was to keep it on the same lot where it originated, but move it closer to Broadway so it can really be front and center.” The decision, he said, was also driven by necessity. Several years ago, an accidental fire in the basement damaged portions of the home’s structure and support beams, according to Ellsworth. Because the house would need to be lifted to repair the foundation and structural components, the project team began evaluating whether it made sense to reposition the building at the same time. “So we asked ourselves, if we are going to have to get it up off the ground and redo the foundation, doesn’t it just make more sense to put this in a spot that’s more prominent on the site?” Semple said. Moving the house closer to the street will allow it to serve as a focal point for the development rather than sit hidden toward the back of the property, according to Ellsworth. The Nathan Smith House weighs approximately 290,000 pounds, or 145 tons. To move it, contractors with Kelly Moving & Rigging first lifted the structure and placed it on a series of steel beams. The house is now in the process of being pushed along those beams using hydraulic rams in a process known as “skating.” The home moves only about five feet at a time, according to Semple.

entry.title: Morrison Center starts on new restrooms, with more projects possible
entry.summary: For the first time in more than 42 years, the Morrison Center for the Performing Arts is undergoing major renovations, and more big changes will be on the way. On Monday, crews broke ground on an estimated $4 million project aimed at expanding and modernizing the building’s restrooms. “For years, our patrons told us what they needed most. This project is our answer,” Morrison Center Executive Director Laura Kendall said. “We are investing in the future of Idaho’s home for the arts — making sure every guest, no matter their age or ability, feels comfortable, welcome, and at home in this building.” The current restrooms were built in 1984, when the building first opened. Lombard Conrad Architects, which designed the original venue, will lead the charge on the renovation. When finished, there will be nearly twice as many restroom stalls, eco-friendly features, and improved crowd flow, among other things. The project is being funded through several sources, including private donations and the Morison Center’s “internal reserves.” A news release says no taxpayer dollars are being used for the renovation. It is expected to be completed this fall, though the stage will be open for select events throughout construction. You can follow along with the project here. Boise State’s newest master plan also calls for consideration of several future upgrades to the Morrison Center, including an expansion of the main entrance to the west along the Boise River Greenbelt with a cafe or food venue. The lobby could also see expansion, with an aim to “improve crowd circulation” during major events. The plan also calls for a possible expansion of the theater, with additional backstage areas, new rehearsal rooms, and “improved production infrastructure to attract larger touring performances.” Concepts show large additions of glass windows, which the plan says could “reinforce interior‑exterior connectivity through daylight views and integrated gathering spaces.” We need your support. BoiseDev provides fact-based reporting to our community free. It''s only possible with support from readers. Make a donation of any size here Or become a member for great benefits. The master plan also calls for growing an arts program for Kindergarten through 12th-grade students, hosting additional city and regional cultural events, and more. The plan also calls for an upgrade to pedestrian connections to the building, with construction set to begin next year. “Making the perimeter of the Morrison Center for the Performing Arts a more welcoming and accessible environment is a project that will help reach the long–term vision of a campus that is connected and easy to navigate,” the plan document notes. “The center’s location highlights the importance of integrating cultural destinations into campus planning, and these enhancements have been identified for future integration.” Boise State is currently in the process of gaining final approvals for the master plan from the City of Boise and the Idaho State Board of Education. Advertise

entry.title: From the Boise Bench to Flavortown 🔥: Local chef competes on Guy’s Grocery Games
entry.summary: A Boise chef made his way to Flavortown, and won big. Susina Chef Alex Cardoza made his first appearance on Guy’s Grocery Games on Wednesday night, a Food Network show where chefs compete in grocery store-themed cooking contests. The episode, called Fan Write-In, featured two contests selected by fans. Cardoza co-owns Susina, a local restaurant on the Boise Bench, with his wife, Tara Morgan. Susina is a pre-fixe supper club with a European-American, seasonal menu. BoiseDev Eats wrote a restaurant spotlight on Susina in 2024. Cardoza was also a semifinalist for a James Beard Award this year in the Best Chef, Mountain Division category, as BoiseDev Eats reported. Cardoza said that the opportunity to appear on Guy’s Grocery Games first came about during the pandemic in 2020, but the timing wasn’t right. He reached out again a few years later, after Cardoza and Morgan opened Susina. “I sent them a message and said, ‘Hey, it’s been a couple of years, but I like the show. I’d like to be on it,’ and they called me back,” Cardoza said. Cardoza said the entire experience of being on Guy’s Grocery Games was “surreal.” “I didn’t get confirmed for the show until five days from filming. Then they flew me to California, we filmed the show in a day, and then they sent me home,” he said. “Then it was 18 months before they aired this episode.” The episode, which aired last night, featured two challenges. The first required the chefs to cook a signature dish using only processed and canned foods. The second required each chef to cook a “Winner, winner chicken dinner,” with a catch: after the chefs shopped for their dish, they were told they had to switch carts. “It was interesting, because I went in there with an idea, with a handful of recipes,” he said. “… Then nothing that I prepared for mattered in the end, because I didn’t even get to pick my own food, so that made it kind of fun. I was like, ‘Alright, well, this is what’s happening. I’m gonna roll with it.’” We need your support. BoiseDev provides fact-based reporting to our community free. It''s only possible with support from readers. Make a donation of any size here Or become a member for great benefits. Cardoza ended up winning the episode and taking home a $14,950 check. “I’m going home with almost $15,000 that I’m going to invest in the restaurant,” Cardoza said. To see Cardoza in action, the episode is re-airing on Food Network on Sunday, June 14, at 2 p.m. You can also watch it on the Food Network site here (you have to connect your TV provider account). It will also be available on Hulu, HBOMax, and Discovery+ at some point — search for Season 42, Episode 1 ‘Fan Write-In.’ Advertise

entry.title: Boise City Council decides on Interfaith, again, considering documents omitted from prior hearing
entry.summary: Interfaith Sanctuary’s State St. shelter will continue operations under the same set of conditions following a decision by the Boise City Council on Tuesday evening. The council voted 4-1 to uphold its decision in September 2025 that approved a conditional use permit for the shelter’s relocation to State St. from Shoreline Dr. Council Member Luci Willits cast the sole dissenting vote. Council President Pro Tem Kathy Corless was absent. Following the September decision, the Veterans Park Neighborhood Association filed for judicial review, arguing that certain evidence was not available to the council for review ahead of the September hearing. Ada County District Court Judge Lynn Norton ordered that the case be remanded to the council so that its members could decide whether to augment the record with the additional materials. The items for the council’s consideration Tuesday included correspondence between VPNA and Boise Planning and Development Services staff; information on crime from the Boise Police Department; and spreadsheets showing calls for service within specific areas of the city. The council’s September vote followed the conditional use permit’s approval by the planning and zoning commission in July 2025. In July, the commission approved the conditional use permit and denied a requested landscaping variance, according to a memo from the city. VPNA then appealed the CUP approval to the council. Boise Senior Planner Josh Wilson told the council Tuesday evening that some of the materials the court required for council review were not available before the September hearing due to an error in a hyperlink in the council report for the meeting. To share the more than 24,000 pages of documents associated with the September hearing, city staff uploaded them to Google Drive and created hyperlinks in the report linking to specific documents. However, the hyperlink for Appendix E in the report inadvertently linked to materials in Appendix D, Wilson said. That prompted the court to remand the case to council to consider augmenting the record with materials from the accidentally omitted materials in Appendix E, he said. Tuesday’s proceedings were not a public hearing, and as a result, only a representative from VPNA and one from the shelter were allowed time to present information. We need your support. BoiseDev provides fact-based reporting to our community free. It''s only possible with support from readers. Make a donation of any size here Or become a member for great benefits. VPNA President Katy Decker said the materials missing for the council’s consideration in September 2025 included information about how Interfaith Sanctuary’s Shoreline Dr. location had impacted surrounding areas. Decker said that this included the number of calls for service within 300 feet of the shelter related to individuals who had sought the shelter’s services. Decker also presented information about the types of crimes associated with calls to police while homeless individuals were temporarily housed at downtown hotels during the COVID-19 pandemic in 2020. Some of these crimes included theft of hotel materials, drug use and possession, and reports of verbal abuse. Decker said that while shelter representatives have indicated they are implementing an array of mitigation measures to protect the surrounding community, she said the hotel issues indicate a need for more stringent requirements in the shelter’s conditional use permit for State St. “The omitted data underline that there’s ample evidence for the potential of Interfaith’s operation to impact the surrounding area,” Decker said. “We’re asking for the conditions of approval to be updated to at minimum reflect the mitigation measures that Interfaith has claimed they are implementing, which are not reflected in the conditions of approval.” Decker expressed concern that the shelter’s permit does not limit it from expanding its population density in the future or restricting it to men only in the future, which she said is associated with higher levels of crime. She also said that the shelter only encourages guests to participate in certain programs, but does not require it. Geoff Wardle, legal counsel for Interfaith Sanctuary, said the matter before the council was an attempt to relitigate the permit. Wardle said that although some 300 pages of spreadsheets weren’t available ahead of the fall council hearing, the council made a sound decision after considering testimony from Decker and other members of the public about how the shelter would impact the surrounding area. The council then “articulated why you didn’t accept that argument,” and adopted conditions when approving the project, he said. “There is no reason to reopen this matter,” Wardle said. “There is nothing that has been presented to you tonight in the augmented materials that dictate any response, any change to the decision you made.” Conditions approved by the council at the September hearing include having the shelter open 24 hours rather than requiring guests to leave during the day, a requirement to provide case management to connect guests to other social services, and preventing the discharge of shelter guests from 7 p.m. to 7 a.m., as BoiseDev reported. The council voted 4-1 to uphold the decision and keep the associated record as is.

entry.title: Boise ice cream shop to open second location
entry.summary: A local soft serve company is expanding to Eagle. One year after opening its first location on State St. in Boise, Soft Landing will open up its second location at 55 E. State St. in Eagle, inside The Hemingway. The shop is planned to open later in June, according to Soft Landing owner Katie Fourcin. “We have been looking around Treasure Valley for the next best place to open, and this opportunity came up in downtown Eagle, which is just such a great community,” Fourcin said. “It’s a really thriving commercial area with a lot of other small businesses, and so we just felt like it’d be a great fit.” The new store will greet customers with the same pastel concept and soft serve flavors familiar at the Boise store. New offerings will also be available. According to Fourcin, Soft Landing’s Eagle location will have two vegan flavors, compared to Boise’s one. She said there will be a coconut milk-based flavor and a sorbet. Also new at the Eagle shop will be “clean” dirty sodas. Fourcin said this is Soft Landing’s take on the soda trend, where people add creamers and syrups to soda. Fourcin said what makes her soda “clean” is her use of organic soda, housemade creamers, and all-natural syrups. Customers can also add ice cream to the sodas to make a float. Fourcin said she didn’t have an official opening date, but one will be announced soon. She said everything is on track to open this month. “We’re super excited to join the Eagle community,” Fourcin said. Soft Landing joins Vessel Kitchen, Siren Song Winemaker’s Loft and Bistro, Lions Den Toys & Books, and Indigo Boutique as the tenants of the new State Street building. Advertise

entry.title: From the Boise Bench to Flavortown 🔥: Local chef competes on Guy’s Grocery Games
Boise ice cream shop to open second location
No. No. No. No. Yes. Nampa lands on new mayor after numerous votes, possible open meetings law violation
‘Not a one-trick pony’: Idaho wine of the year shines for versatility, longevity
Nampa boba and dessert shop expanding into Boise this summer
Ada clerk rules on challenge to Davidson’s residency
You Asked: Why were there over 290,000 fewer eligible voters in Idaho for this past election?
You Asked: Why Bigfoot was on Ada County’s ballots & I Voted stickers?
Voter turnout up across Ada County despite multiple districts with unopposed candidates
In aftermath of ‘Everyone is Welcome,’ one W. Ada incumbent loses seat, one holds on
Vietnamese restaurant concept to open in Meridian
entry.summary: There’s a new Vietnamese restaurant heading into the former Urban Fox Meridian location. Xin Chao is opening at 2970 N Eagle Rd in Meridian. Business owner, An Van Nguyen, plans to open this new concept with the same building structure as its previous owners, but make some adjustments to the inside of the space. According to Meridian permits, Nguyen is proposing the idea of having a rooftop seating area in addition to classic indoor seating and dining services. BoiseDev has reached out to the owner to find out more information, but did not hear back from Nguyen. A kitchen hood is being installed in preparation for the cuisine that will be served along with a food prep and support area that is about 915 square feet of the building space. Permits suggest the kitchen area is receiving some upgrades from what it was used for with Urban Fox. The existing wall inside the structure is to remain the same along with the existing equipment and service counter that Urban Fox was operating with. The restrooms are also remaining alongside the transition into Xin Chao. Advertise

entry.title: From the Boise Bench to Flavortown 🔥: Local chef competes on Guy’s Grocery Games
entry.summary: A Boise chef made his way to Flavortown, and won big. Susina Chef Alex Cardoza made his first appearance on Guy’s Grocery Games on Wednesday night, a Food Network show where chefs compete in grocery store-themed cooking contests. The episode, called Fan Write-In, featured two contests selected by fans. Cardoza co-owns Susina, a local restaurant on the Boise Bench, with his wife, Tara Morgan. Susina is a pre-fixe supper club with a European-American, seasonal menu. BoiseDev Eats wrote a restaurant spotlight on Susina in 2024. Cardoza was also a semifinalist for a James Beard Award this year in the Best Chef, Mountain Division category, as BoiseDev Eats reported. Cardoza said that the opportunity to appear on Guy’s Grocery Games first came about during the pandemic in 2020, but the timing wasn’t right. He reached out again a few years later, after Cardoza and Morgan opened Susina. “I sent them a message and said, ‘Hey, it’s been a couple of years, but I like the show. I’d like to be on it,’ and they called me back,” Cardoza said. Cardoza said the entire experience of being on Guy’s Grocery Games was “surreal.” “I didn’t get confirmed for the show until five days from filming. Then they flew me to California, we filmed the show in a day, and then they sent me home,” he said. “Then it was 18 months before they aired this episode.” The episode, which aired last night, featured two challenges. The first required the chefs to cook a signature dish using only processed and canned foods. The second required each chef to cook a “Winner, winner chicken dinner,” with a catch: after the chefs shopped for their dish, they were told they had to switch carts. “It was interesting, because I went in there with an idea, with a handful of recipes,” he said. “… Then nothing that I prepared for mattered in the end, because I didn’t even get to pick my own food, so that made it kind of fun. I was like, ‘Alright, well, this is what’s happening. I’m gonna roll with it.’” We need your support. BoiseDev provides fact-based reporting to our community free. It''s only possible with support from readers. Make a donation of any size here Or become a member for great benefits. Cardoza ended up winning the episode and taking home a $14,950 check. “I’m going home with almost $15,000 that I’m going to invest in the restaurant,” Cardoza said. To see Cardoza in action, the episode is re-airing on Food Network on Sunday, June 14, at 2 p.m. You can also watch it on the Food Network site here (you have to connect your TV provider account). It will also be available on Hulu, HBOMax, and Discovery+ at some point — search for Season 42, Episode 1 ‘Fan Write-In.’ Advertise

entry.title: New indoor golf training facility opening in Meridian
entry.summary: This website uses a security service to protect against malicious bots. This page is displayed while the website verifies you are not a bot. Your browser extensions or network settings have blocked the security verification process required by boisedev.com. To resolve this, try the following steps: If these steps do not resolve the issue, refer to Cloudflare''s troubleshooting documentation for more help. For detailed guidance on how to disable your browser extensions or check your network settings, refer to your browser or device’s documentation.

entry.title: Boise ice cream shop to open second location
entry.summary: A local soft serve company is expanding to Eagle. One year after opening its first location on State St. in Boise, Soft Landing will open up its second location at 55 E. State St. in Eagle, inside The Hemingway. The shop is planned to open later in June, according to Soft Landing owner Katie Fourcin. “We have been looking around Treasure Valley for the next best place to open, and this opportunity came up in downtown Eagle, which is just such a great community,” Fourcin said. “It’s a really thriving commercial area with a lot of other small businesses, and so we just felt like it’d be a great fit.” The new store will greet customers with the same pastel concept and soft serve flavors familiar at the Boise store. New offerings will also be available. According to Fourcin, Soft Landing’s Eagle location will have two vegan flavors, compared to Boise’s one. She said there will be a coconut milk-based flavor and a sorbet. Also new at the Eagle shop will be “clean” dirty sodas. Fourcin said this is Soft Landing’s take on the soda trend, where people add creamers and syrups to soda. Fourcin said what makes her soda “clean” is her use of organic soda, housemade creamers, and all-natural syrups. Customers can also add ice cream to the sodas to make a float. Fourcin said she didn’t have an official opening date, but one will be announced soon. She said everything is on track to open this month. “We’re super excited to join the Eagle community,” Fourcin said. Soft Landing joins Vessel Kitchen, Siren Song Winemaker’s Loft and Bistro, Lions Den Toys & Books, and Indigo Boutique as the tenants of the new State Street building. Advertise

entry.title: Nampa boba and dessert shop expanding into Boise this summer
entry.summary: A well-known boba and dessert shop, located at 2585 Caldwell Blvd in Nampa, is expanding to Boise and is set to open this summer. Thirsty Shack is a one-stop shop for creative boba flavors, tea, crepes, fruit desserts, and more. And it’s best known for bringing viral social media desserts to the Treasure Valley. “I wanted to come to Boise for the longest time ever,” said Adela Jaramillo, Owner of Thirsty Shack. “But it’s extremely expensive for a small business to venture into that area.” Jaramillo tells BoiseDev that she always wanted to establish a successful small business first and then eventually grow it into a Boise location. The new location will mirror the original Nampa location; however, Jaramillo is introducing one item that its Nampa shop doesn’t sell: European-inspired pastries. Jaramillo said the majority of the pastries will be eclairs and tarts, “many of which are inspired by the sweets I grew up loving throughout my life.” “At the end of each week, we’re gonna implement a new pastry from where I’m from to see how the community likes it and what they think of it,” Jaramillo said. Thirsty Shack’s new Boise shop is located at 1525 S. Five Mile in Boise and is expected to be open by mid-July. Significant upgrades are being made to the inside and outside of the new space, but there is no major reconstruction planned for the building. “I have a lot of customers who drive all the way from Boise to Nampa to buy our drinks, but I think I’m more excited about bringing in the pastries, because that’s something that we don’t offer right now at the Nampa location,” Jaramillo said she is excited to expand even further into the Treasure Valley. We need your support. BoiseDev provides fact-based reporting to our community free. It''s only possible with support from readers. Make a donation of any size here Or become a member for great benefits. “This new chapter feels incredibly personal and so special to me, and I cannot wait to share it with all of you,” Jaramillo said. Advertise

entry.title: ‘Fun, glam shopping experience’: New women’s clothing and accessories boutique opening in Caldwell
entry.summary: A new women’s clothing and accessories boutique is opening in Caldwell today. Beyond 208 Boutique carries a variety of items, including western-inspired styles, graphic tees, handbags, wallets, jewelry, hats, accessories, clear stadium bags, trucker hats, and more. “Beyond 208 Boutique was created from my passion for fashion, confidence, and helping women feel beautiful and empowered,” Beyond 208 Boutique owner Sheri Adams said. “I wanted to create more than just a boutique — I wanted to build a fun, glam shopping experience that combines western style, designer-inspired fashion, trendy accessories, and bougie vibes all in one place.” The store also carries add-on accessories like bag charms, hat bands, and customizable accessories that help customers express their own style. “We want customers to feel like they’re shopping with their besties — whether they’re finding the perfect handbag, putting together an outfit, customizing items at our trucker hat bar, or discovering new accessories,” Adams said. “It’s all about confidence, style, and having fun while shopping.” Adams says she chose Caldwell because she saw an opportunity to bring something unique and exciting to the area. “Caldwell has an amazing sense of community, and I wanted to create a boutique destination where women can shop trendy fashion and accessories locally while enjoying a fun, elevated atmosphere,” Adams said. Beyond 208 Boutique’s grand opening is today, May 30, from 11 a.m. – 6 p.m. at 2404 Cleveland Blvd. in Caldwell. For additional opening updates, follow Beyond 208 Boutique on Instagram. Advertise

entry.title: ‘Hat for every head’: Custom hat maker opens storefront in downtown Boise
entry.summary: A new store in downtown Boise is offering custom hats for everyday wear and special occasions. After years of crafting hats out of his home, hat maker Toby Robin opened a storefront, Reverent Hats, in January 2025. “I hear a lot of folks say they ‘don’t look good in hats’ or they’re ‘not able to pull off the look,''” Robin said. “To that I say, challenge accepted. I truly believe there’s a hat for EVERY head and I’m here to help make them.” After 20 years in graphic design, Robin found himself craving a more hands-on form of creativity. That desire, along with a visit back to his hometown in Wyoming, led him to start Reverent Hats in 2021. “With hatmaking, I can collaborate with clients and help them express themselves by making them a timeless and authentic hat,” Robin said. “More than an accessory, our hats are an extension of those wearing them.” Robin said customers can shop for ready-made hats in-store or stop in for a fitting or consultation — no appointment needed. Each hat is made in the store, giving visitors a chance to see the process firsthand. “Every hat is blocked, shaped, stretched, sewn, and decorated by hand. From hand-stitched brim edge binding to hatbands made from horse tack, the hatter’s hand is always visible and an intricate part of each hat,” Robin said. “We want our hats to look handmade. There’s a time and place for big box perfection — this ain’t it.” Robin said his customers range from working cowboys/cowgirls to city folks looking for an everyday hat. “The one thing they all have in common is the appreciation of craftsmanship and the custom experience. I hear it time and again from my customers: once they have a custom hat, they can’t go back to wearing off-the-shelf hats,” Robin said. “Not only do they get a perfectly fitting hat, but they also collaborate on every detail. It really is a unique experience.” We need your support. BoiseDev provides fact-based reporting to our community free. It''s only possible with support from readers. Make a donation of any size here Or become a member for great benefits. Reverent Hats is open at 106 N. 6th St. in Boise, Monday through Friday from 10 a.m. – 6 p.m. To see more of Robin’s work, head to Reverent Hat’s Instagram page here. Advertise

entry.title: Treasure hunting on State Street: Boise antique shop offers vintage finds from 25+ vendors
entry.summary: For the last seven years, an antique and collectible shop has been featuring a variety of items from over 25 vendors. Sherri Petterson opened State Street Antiques, located at 4718 W. State St., in 2019. “State Street Antiques opened in the spring of 2019. Sherri wanted to share her experience, passion, and love of antiques that she has had for the past 50 years,” Social Media Manager Sarah Petterson said. The shop carries a variety of items, including clothing and collectibles that span multiple decades. They also have handmade items, records, taxidermy, and rocks/crystals. “When the opportunity arose for Sherri to create her own shop and an environment where others could share that passion alongside her, she did not hesitate to reach out,” Petterson said. “This sharing includes both the avid antique lovers and the vendors themselves who participate in the store’s spaces.” State Street Antiques is open Monday through Saturday from 10 a.m. – 6 p.m., and Sundays from 11 a.m. to 5 p.m. “People love the variety of items, the quality of customer service we provide, and a cozy environment to shop in,” Petterson said. “The staff, especially the owner, Sherri, loves connecting and helping customers find the perfect treasure for themself or others.” To check out some of the items featured in the shop, check out State Street Antiques’ Facebook page here. Advertise

entry.title: ‘Needed niche’: Consignment bridal shop opens on the Boise Bench
entry.summary: The average wedding dress costs about $2,100, according to The Knot 2026 Real Weddings Study, which surveyed over 10,000 US couples married in 2025. That’s part of what inspired Alexandra Garnica to open a consignment bridal shop on the Boise Bench, where dresses start at $600. The Dress Collective — which both sells and buys wedding gowns — opened its doors at the corner of Emerald and Orchard in March. “We know the modern bride wants a stunning designer gown at an affordable price. We also know past brides sometimes want an option to sell their gown after their wedding to let another bride enjoy as much as they did,” Garnica said. “That’s why we’re called a collective, brides helping other brides have their dream wedding dress for their dream day.” In addition to buying wedding gowns, the Dress Collective is also partnering with Kate’s Bridal Cottage and BeSpoke Bride, who consign samples at the shop. “I love the idea of bridal shops helping other bridal shops as well,” Garnica said. “We help bridal shops make room for their new inventory while giving brides an opportunity to purchase a designer gown at a discounted price.” The Dress Collective’s collection features designers from its approved list. Prices range from $600 to $3,500, with the bulk of gowns in the $1,000- $1,500 range. The shop also has a small collection of mini gowns available for rehearsals, bridal showers, etc. “So far, everyone has said this has been such a needed niche in our community, specifically for brides hoping to shop consignment and also brides who want to spend about $1,000,” Garnica said. “We have received a very positive and warm welcome to the bridal space by customers and other bridal shops alike.” For those in the market looking to sell a wedding gown, a consignment form is available on the shop’s website. We need your support. BoiseDev provides fact-based reporting to our community free. It''s only possible with support from readers. Make a donation of any size here Or become a member for great benefits. “Whether you’re ready to find your dream dress or are ready to send your gown on to its next bride, The Dress Collective is here to celebrate every dress and every bride’s story,” Garnica said. “We’re passionate about making bridal shopping more accessible, more sustainable — and just a little more magical.” The Dress Collective, open at 4919 W. Emerald St., is open by appointment for those who want to either shop for or sell a gown. Advertise

entry.title: New spot for kids who like sand… and trucks… now open
entry.summary: A new construction-themed indoor sandbox and cafe is opening this summer in Boise. Sunrise Digger Zone is designed for kids ages 1-10 to dig, build, and use their imagination. “We truly believe this will be a special place for the community,” owner Nate Schwegman said. “Our goal isn’t just to create a fun space, it’s to create a place where families connect, kids create memories, and people feel welcomed every time they walk through the door.” Sunrise Digger Zone will have a large indoor sandbox experience filled with construction toys and hands-on digging areas. There will also be a cafe area for parents to hang out while their kids play. “Families can expect a clean, welcoming, and thoughtfully designed space where kids can play freely, and parents can actually enjoy being there too,” Schwegman said. “We’ve created a balance between active play and a relaxing café-style environment, so it feels just as good for parents as it does for kids.” Schwegman said maintaining a clean environment is a big priority. “We have a structured cleaning process that includes daily maintenance, routine sanitizing of high-touch areas, and ongoing care of the sand itself using safe, kid-friendly methods,” Schwegman said. “As parents, we’ve designed this with the same level of care we would want for our own children.” Schwegman said Sunrise Digger Zone will open sometime this summer at 2505 W. Chinden Blvd. To follow along with opening updates, head to Sunrise Digger Zone’s Instagram page. Advertise

entry.title: Vietnamese restaurant concept to open in Meridian
From the Boise Bench to Flavortown 🔥: Local chef competes on Guy’s Grocery Games
Boise ice cream shop to open second location
‘One of the biggest attractions’ in Idaho: Vision for Indian Creek could add dozens of projects, including whitewater park, promenade, more
Nampa boba and dessert shop expanding into Boise this summer
‘Fun, glam shopping experience’: New women’s clothing and accessories boutique opening in Caldwell
Church of Jesus Christ confirms location of Caldwell Temple
Nearly 1,000 high-end homes approved for Caldwell
From high school to stud finders: old building finds new owner
You Asked: What’s going into the old Caldwell Rite Aid?
McCall leases land to developer for low-income housing
entry.summary: An apartment complex could soon be built on the City of McCall’s downtown campus. On Thursday, the McCall City Council approved leasing nearly a half-acre of its downtown campus to LEAP Housing, a Boise nonprofit focused on developing affordable housing. Plans are still being finalized, but McCall Housing Program Manager Bri Zak said she expects the complex to include 30 to 40 apartments with income qualifications and rental rate caps. “I think it’s just a super opportunity for the city,” Council Member Lyle Nelson said before joining a unanimous vote to approve the lease. The lease covers the northwest corner of the city’s 4.7-acre campus, carving out unused ground fronting First Street just north of the McCall Senior Center. The lease carries a 75-year term at a rate of $1 per year. It would expire after two years, however, if LEAP is unable to gain approval for federal low-income housing tax credits. The tax credits are administered by the U.S. Department of Housing and Urban Development through the Idaho Housing and Finance Association. The 2027 application deadline is in August. If awarded, the tax credits would subsidize construction of the apartments in exchange for rental rate caps and income qualifications for tenants. The credits are expected to limit occupancy of the apartments to households that earn about $64,000 per year or less. That income limit would be lower for households with fewer than four people, under HUD rules. LEAP also still needs to submit plans for the apartment complex to the city for approval. That process would include public hearings before the McCall Planning and Zoning Commission and the McCall City Council. The apartments could rise as high as 50 feet — the building height limit on the property under city zoning laws. Bart Cochran, the CEO and Founder of LEAP, previously suggested that the first floor of the apartments could be devoted to parking to offset the loss of existing parking spaces on the property. Last year the city council directed Zak to pursue low-income housing partnerships with LEAP on two city-owned parcels, including the civic campus, which is also home to City Hall and the McCall Public Library. No paywall. No clickbait. No AI. No corporate owner. Your support makes Valley Lookout possible. Make a donation of any size here The other parcel — a two-acre lot on Flynn Lane near Ponderosa State Park — was targeted for 16 affordable homes that would be available for purchase. The Flynn Lane project stalled, however, after it was determined the site could only get three sewer connection permits amid capacity problems in the sewer system serving the city. The Springs, a 72-apartment development completed in 2016 off Deinhard Lane, marked the last time the tax credits were used to build housing in McCall. The apartments are currently full, with a long waitlist. The McCall Area Comprehensive Plan, the city’s Local Housing Action Plan, and a 2019 Civic Campus Master Plan each call for the development of local housing on city-owned land. LEAP Housing has previously built a total of 186 affordable units across 13 developments. In 2025, the nonprofit was awarded about $1.4 million in tax credits for affordable housing projects in Boise and Mountain Home.

entry.title: Gas station + BBQ = best friends: Local barbecue joint expands to Meridian
entry.summary: Brisket, pulled pork, and baby back ribs will soon be available in North Meridian. Neighbor Tim’s BBQ is planning a new location at 1410 W. McMillan Rd. It is taking over the space next to the Stinker’s Store that previously housed Gandolfo’s. “I think it’s just great. McMillan and Linder is full of houses. Rocky Mountain High School is just right down the street. There’s all sorts of other eateries there,” said Neighbor Tim’s BBQ owner Tim Goebel. “Then being attached to the Stinker gas station, we can obviously partner up with that, where they’re bringing in guests for us and we’re bringing in guests for them.” There will be a walk-through between Stinker’s and Neighbor Tims, and Goebel is excited to be connected with the gas station. “I’ve always felt like a gas station and a barbecue spot are like best friends, you know what I mean,” he said. “So I’ve always had this dream of like being attached with a gas station… I just think it’s awesome.” Goebel said the menu will be consistent with the other two locations, featuring a range of barbecued and smoked meats, including brisket, pulled pork, German sausage, baby back ribs, and BBQ chicken. There are also several classic barbecue sides like mac and cheese, coleslaw, baked beans, and potato salad. The new location will feature indoor and outdoor seating, along with a drive-thru. Neighbor Tim’s is celebrating five years in business. BoiseDev reported when Neighbor Tim’s opened its first location at 7802 W. Goddard Rd. in Boise in 2021. It opened its second location in The Warehouse in 2022 as one of the original residents. Goebel said he is planning minimal updates to the new space and expects to open by mid-September. We need your support. BoiseDev provides fact-based reporting to our community free. It''s only possible with support from readers. Make a donation of any size here Or become a member for great benefits. Goebel is also still on the hunt for a new location for Tim’s Burgers, a concept he opened at The Boardwalk in early 2025 and closed abruptly in October. Tim’s Burgers and Bao Boi, both former tenants at The Boardwalk, are currently in the midst of lawsuits against Crispy Investments, their landlord, alleging lease issues and defamation, as BoiseDev reported. Goebel cited exclusives as the main challenge in finding a new location. “We’re still marinating on a place for that. The difficulty I’m having is that the majority of corners that are available for me to put a burger spot, have a burger spot already in that corner, which is fine. I’ll compete, that doesn’t matter to me,” he said. “It’s more they have exclusives on shopping centers, so I can’t come in because there’s another burger joint that has the exclusive on the shopping center.” He is still optimistic and hopes to find the right spot for Tim’s Burgers. “I still have aspirations to do that. It was a great concept and a great product. I hear praise about it all the time,” Goebel said. “… We’re still on the hunt for the best corner we can find.”

entry.title: Vietnamese restaurant concept to open in Meridian
entry.summary: There’s a new Vietnamese restaurant heading into the former Urban Fox Meridian location. Xin Chao is opening at 2970 N Eagle Rd in Meridian. Business owner, An Van Nguyen, plans to open this new concept with the same building structure as its previous owners, but make some adjustments to the inside of the space. According to Meridian permits, Nguyen is proposing the idea of having a rooftop seating area in addition to classic indoor seating and dining services. BoiseDev has reached out to the owner to find out more information, but did not hear back from Nguyen. A kitchen hood is being installed in preparation for the cuisine that will be served along with a food prep and support area that is about 915 square feet of the building space. Permits suggest the kitchen area is receiving some upgrades from what it was used for with Urban Fox. The existing wall inside the structure is to remain the same along with the existing equipment and service counter that Urban Fox was operating with. The restrooms are also remaining alongside the transition into Xin Chao.

entry.title: Mix of townhomes, apartments planned near growing Meridian corner
entry.summary: More housing could become available on a busy Meridian corner. Initial documents filed with the City of Meridian show early plans for a 250-unit housing development just north of the intersection of Eagle Rd. and Victory Rd. According to the early concept plan, Emblem Meridian would feature a mix of apartments and townhomes on 13 acres of land. The site plan shows 113 one-bedroom, 86 two-bedroom, and 51 three-bedroom units. About 520 parking spaces and 92 garages are also planned for the site. Additionally, the concept plan shows a clubhouse, pool, dog park, and grassy area. Preapplication documents show Lennar-backed Quarterra, which focuses on multi-family projects, is involved with Emblem. Emblem would border the retirement community, The Pointe at Meridian, and also connect to the roads of the surrounding neighborhoods if it were to be built out according to the concept plan. In recent years, Primrose School, Starbucks, and a retail building have all set up shop on the same corner. Idaho Fitness Factory and Black Rock Coffee Bar have also moved in across the street. The project is still in its early stages and would need the developers to submit an official application to the city and go through the municipal approval process before anything could move forward.

entry.title: Boise ice cream shop to open second location
entry.summary: A local soft serve company is expanding to Eagle. One year after opening its first location on State St. in Boise, Soft Landing will open up its second location at 55 E. State St. in Eagle, inside The Hemingway. The shop is planned to open later in June, according to Soft Landing owner Katie Fourcin. “We have been looking around Treasure Valley for the next best place to open, and this opportunity came up in downtown Eagle, which is just such a great community,” Fourcin said. “It’s a really thriving commercial area with a lot of other small businesses, and so we just felt like it’d be a great fit.” The new store will greet customers with the same pastel concept and soft serve flavors familiar at the Boise store. New offerings will also be available. According to Fourcin, Soft Landing’s Eagle location will have two vegan flavors, compared to Boise’s one. She said there will be a coconut milk-based flavor and a sorbet. Also new at the Eagle shop will be “clean” dirty sodas. Fourcin said this is Soft Landing’s take on the soda trend, where people add creamers and syrups to soda. Fourcin said what makes her soda “clean” is her use of organic soda, housemade creamers, and all-natural syrups. Customers can also add ice cream to the sodas to make a float. Fourcin said she didn’t have an official opening date, but one will be announced soon. She said everything is on track to open this month. “We’re super excited to join the Eagle community,” Fourcin said. Soft Landing joins Vessel Kitchen, Siren Song Winemaker’s Loft and Bistro, Lions Den Toys & Books, and Indigo Boutique as the tenants of the new State Street building.

entry.title: Boise apartments proposed where lack of roads prohibits development due to fire risk. There might be a workaround
entry.summary: A Boise developer hopes to build nearly 200 apartments in an area the city has said it wouldn’t allow additional development. Hawkins Companies applied to build 198 apartments on E. Columbia Rd., on land owned by the Carson Close Family Trust, which is currently listed for sale. The project is between the Painted Ridge and Sunny Ridge Developments in an area north of Micron Technology’s Boise facilities. Columbia Rd. can only be accessed from Federal Way, with the other end turning into a road that stops at a dead end. As BoiseDev reported, the City of Boise’s adopted fire code typically bars more than 60 housing units from being built in an area without a secondary access for fire crews. There are currently more than 100 homes in the existing subdivisions. City of Boise spokesperson Maria Ortega told BoiseDev earlier this year that the area had reached the maximum number of homes allowed. Micron wants to funnel additional construction traffic onto Technology Way and Columbia Rd., which would then access a Micron parking lot. Micron and the J.R. Simplot Co. own large landholdings in the area and have not been required to build an additional access point, despite Micron’s wish to increase traffic on the road. But there could be a carve-out. If the new multi-family project has fire sprinklers, it may not need the access route. “Multi-family developments on this parcel are governed by 2021 International Fire Code Appendix D106, which requires multi-family developments between 100 and 200 units to either provide a secondary fire access road or fire-sprinkler (sic) all buildings to NFPA 13 or 13R standards,” Hawkins pre-development manager Ethan Mansfield wrote in a letter outlining Hawkins’ request to annex the property into the City of Boise. “We are committed to meeting this standard by providing sprinklers in all buildings.” Beyond fire access, if the project moves forward, it will need to be annexed into the city and go through the city’s design review process. The city’s preliminary notes indicate it may also need to meet other requirements.

entry.title: Five-story building proposed for site of old condemned hotel
entry.summary: Three years after city officials shut down the Travelers Motel for “deplorable” living conditions, a new project could rise up from the ground. According to early planning documents filed with the City of Boise, a five-story apartment building with an affordability component is proposed for the area. The initial paperwork states the project would be a “much-needed redevelopment of this site.” Early concept plans for the site show a five-story building with 97 apartments. Of the apartments, 79 are labeled as studio, 12 as one-bedroom, and six as two-bedroom. Forty-three parking stalls are slated for the building, one of which is designated as motorcycle parking. According to a submitted project narrative, the owner of the property is “going to pursue affordability of at least 25% of the units to qualify for a parking reduction.” The narrative says that the project’s location on Fairview provides tenants with multiple options for “alternative transportation.” Parking would be accessed from Beach St. Affordable housing refers to housing set aside specifically for residents making at or below income levels established by the U.S. Department of Housing and Urban Development. Based on area median income, family size, and rents in the area, the agency sets rent levels for affordable housing projects with the goal of housing costs not exceeding 30% of household income. Housing affordability is relative for everyone depending on their circumstances, but there are legal definitions of what qualifies as affordable housing and how it has to operate in government. The potential plans are the latest in the Travelers Motel redevelopment saga, which started with the motel’s shutdown in 2023. As BoiseDev reported at the time, fire inspectors found black mold, rotting floors, electrical issues, insect infestations, no running water, feces, vomit, and a myriad of other conditions, leading them to classify the motel as “unfit for habitation.” In January, the developer applied for a demolition permit to level the property and start anew, as BoiseDev reported. A chain-link fence with the advertisement for a demolition company has gone up around the property, but the buildings still stand. The project is still in the very early stages and could change. To move forward, the developer would have to submit additional application materials, go through a design review process, and get approval. Rodney + Evans Partners’ Ben Semple told BoiseDev the developer is meeting with the city soon to get initial feedback on the project and plans to host a neighborhood meeting in the near future.

entry.title: Nampa boba and dessert shop expanding into Boise this summer
entry.summary: A well-known boba and dessert shop, located at 2585 Caldwell Blvd in Nampa, is expanding to Boise and is set to open this summer. Thirsty Shack is a one-stop shop for creative boba flavors, tea, crepes, fruit desserts, and more. And it’s best known for bringing viral social media desserts to the Treasure Valley. “I wanted to come to Boise for the longest time ever,” said Adela Jaramillo, Owner of Thirsty Shack. “But it’s extremely expensive for a small business to venture into that area.” Jaramillo tells BoiseDev that she always wanted to establish a successful small business first and then eventually grow it into a Boise location. The new location will mirror the original Nampa location; however, Jaramillo is introducing one item that its Nampa shop doesn’t sell: European-inspired pastries. Jaramillo said the majority of the pastries will be eclairs and tarts, “many of which are inspired by the sweets I grew up loving throughout my life.” “At the end of each week, we’re gonna implement a new pastry from where I’m from to see how the community likes it and what they think of it,” Jaramillo said. Thirsty Shack’s new Boise shop is located at 1525 S. Five Mile in Boise and is expected to be open by mid-July. Significant upgrades are being made to the inside and outside of the new space, but there is no major reconstruction planned for the building. “I have a lot of customers who drive all the way from Boise to Nampa to buy our drinks, but I think I’m more excited about bringing in the pastries, because that’s something that we don’t offer right now at the Nampa location,” Jaramillo said she is excited to expand even further into the Treasure Valley. We need your support. BoiseDev provides fact-based reporting to our community free. It''s only possible with support from readers. Make a donation of any size here Or become a member for great benefits. “This new chapter feels incredibly personal and so special to me, and I cannot wait to share it with all of you,” Jaramillo said.

entry.title: Over 400 homes planned for 123 acres in Meridian
entry.summary: More than 400 additional homes could sprout up along Linder Rd. in South Meridian. Preliminary application documents for the Catalina Commons neighborhood have been filed with the City of Meridian. The early plans show a map featuring 420 homes on over 123 acres located west of Linder Rd. and north of Amity Rd., next to the planned Brundage Estates neighborhood. Lot sizes for the potential subdivision range from 4,390 square feet to 53,668 square feet. According to early planning documents, the neighborhood is split into two housing densities, with 56 larger lots at the southwest corner of the property and 364 smaller lots filling the rest of the neighborhood’s footprint. The larger lots have an average size of 20,649 square feet, while the smaller lots average 5,958 square feet. Property records show the land for Catalina Commons is owned by an LLC tied to Hubble Homes. KM Engineering submitted the preliminary application to the city. A home and a few other structures currently sit on the property. Catalina Commons is still in the early stages. For the homes to materialize, the developer would have to fill out an official application with the City of Meridian and go through the approval process to annex into city limits and build the project.

entry.title: Boise set to spend $1.5 million on empty land by Boise mall
entry.summary: The City of Boise plans to buy a piece of property overlooking Boise Towne Square for affordable housing. As BoiseDev reported, in 2021, CCD Properties applied to the city to build 71 “micro apartments” on a 1.64-acre piece of property at 709 N. Cole Rd. The plan called for the apartments to range from 338 to 542 square feet. The project did not move forward, and CCD sold the site to Aimfield LLC of Midvale, Utah. The site has been listed for sale for more than a year. It was initially priced at $2.2 million, but earlier this year the seller cut the price to $1.6 million, according to a listing on commercial site Crexi. The city is proposing to buy the property for $1.5 million, according to a memo from city attorney Kim Stretch. “This property is located near Boise Towne Square Mall, and transit stops off of Cole Road, which presents an opportunity for an accessible affordable housing project,” Stretch wrote. “The cost of acquisition will come from the Housing and Community Development budget.” If the city council signs off, the city would have a 45-day due diligence period to evaluate the property and would close on the sale next month. The city has increasingly purchased sites and worked with private developers to build affordable housing. It completed several apartment complexes, including The Franklin at Franklin and Orchard, Wilson Station on State St., and others.
